--- 
title: "UvA Intervention Mapping Workbook"
author: "Nicole, GJ & Rob"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
site: bookdown::bookdown_site
output: bookdown::gitbook
documentclass: book
bibliography: [IMuva.bib]
biblio-style: apalike
link-citations: yes
description: "The general aim of the workshop is to practice the steps of the intervention mapping protocol in groups. The structure of the workshop is guided by the IM protocol, as described in the book PLANNING HEALTH PROMOTION PROGRAMS by Bartholomew et al (2016). Below, a detailed outline is presented of working with the Intervention Mapping protocol. For each step of the protocol specific tasks are formulated."
---

# Introduction {-}

This is the Intervention Mapping Work Book. Is is an Open Access book, freely available under the [Creative Commons CC-BY-NC-SA license](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/) at https://im-wb.com. This book is accompanied by an Exercise Book, which is available as a Google Sheet at https://im-wb.com/exercise-book. 

## Aim of the workshop

The general aim of the workshop is to practice the steps of the Intervention Mapping protocol in groups. The structure of the workshop is guided by the IM protocol, as described in the book PLANNING HEALTH PROMOTION PROGRAMS by Bartholomew et al (2016). Below, a detailed outline is presented of working with the Intervention Mapping protocol. For each step of the protocol, specific tasks are formulated.

### Group Work

You are performing the tasks in workgroups.

To collaborate on the exercises in this book, a main document is available as a Google Doc word processor document. Either your course instructors have prepared a version for your group, in which case they will provide the link in your electronic learning environment, or you can copy the [standard read-only version that is available here](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1U5eHyZNc-mCUv1YziEZGyYpaPlcGjiUXwcQ5SYE_Ji4). In addition to providing a structure for the answers you will produce in this course, the main document will also link to a number of Google Sheets that you will use.

From the main document and the associated spreadsheets, you will make selections of the most important progress points, decisions, justifications, and issues. You will regularly present these in the plenary sessions so that you can receive feedback from an IM expert. Whereas the main document is highly structured, following the structure of this book, it is up to you to decide what to present and how. We recommend using Google Slides to enable smooth synchronous collaboration, but if you prefer, you can also use other applications such as Microsoft PowerPoint, Apple Keynote, or Prezi. Note that you will have to turn the visual support you produce for your presentations in the PDF format, regardless of which application you choose to use.

By Friday, your group will have gone through all the steps, and you will have prepared a full main document as well as a full presentation of the most important points. You will present this final product on Friday, and you will receive one more round of expert feedback on the results of your intervention mapping and will have time to process that feedback to improve the plans. The final set of documents (the presentation, the main document, and the associated spreadsheets) must be handed in at the end of the workshop week (deadline Friday 18:00 CEST).

## Core processes {#core-processes}

In Intervention Mapping, questions are answered using the core processes. These processes are a systematic approach to obtaining answers that are theory- and evidence based, and allow balancing pragmatic considerations such as resource availability (time, funding) with optimizing answer quality and scientific integrity. These are explained in Chapter 1 of the Intervention Mapping book. The Core Processes are:

1. Pose the question(s).
2. Brainstorm potential answers to establish what the planning group already knows and draft a provisional list of answers.
3. Review the scientific literature for answers based on empirical evidence and/or theory.
4. Review applicable theories to expand the potential answers.
5. Assess whether new data are needed, and if so, collect and process these.
6. Develop a semi-definite list of answers.

Note that like most aspects of IM, application of the core processes is iterative; therefore, the semi-definite list produced in the sixth phase can always be revised.

In this course, there is no time to comprehensively apply these core processes. Therefore, instead, you will mostly just brainstorm potential answers. You can supplement the brainstorm with the literature that is provided and with quick internet searches, but make sure to not spend too much on obtaining the best answers.

It is important that you understand which choices you have to make and how you can justify those choices. The quality of the evidence and theory you use is crucial in real life, but not important in this course.

![CC-BY-NC-SA](img/by-nc-sa.eu.png)
